data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures rise ahead of PCE, GDP
data.description:
Stock index futures rose on Friday as investors prepared for a busy morning of economic data, with GDP and PCE figures topping the calendar.

Dow futures (INDU) +0.3%. S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.3%, and Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.3%.

Durable goods orders and GDP will land at the same time Wednesday morning. Orders are expected to rise 0.5% after falling 1.4% on weak aircraft orders a month earlier, while the consensus sees no revision for GDP.

Personal income and outlays will also be released simultaneously, with income expected to outpace spending—income up 0.5% and spending up 0.3% on the month.

The JOLTS and the consumer sentiment reports will land during market hours. 

U.S. Treasury yields ticked higher across the curve. The 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) stood at 3.75%, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose to 4.27%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) climbed to 4.90%.

Top gainers premarket included Darden Restaurants (DRI) +5.66%, Mosaic (MOS) +3.64%, and CF Industries (CF) +3.04%. Decliners included Ulta Beauty (ULTA) -8.92%, Adobe (ADBE) -8.71% and Insulet (PODD) -3.91%.

data.title: GSK wins FDA label expansion for RSV vaccine, Arexvy
data.description:
GSK (GSK) announced on Friday that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration ((FDA)) has approved a label expansion for its Arexvy vaccine against the Respiratory Syncytial Virus ((RSV)) to allow its use for those aged 18 to 49 years.

Accordingly, the single-dose shot will be available in the U.S. for those in that age group who are at an elevated risk for lower respiratory tract disease ((LRTD)) caused by the RSV.

Arexvy, which contains an adjuvant licensed from Antigenics Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of Agenus (AGEN), became the first U.S.-cleared shot for RSV in May 2023, as the FDA approved it for people aged 60 years and older to prevent LRTD caused by the virus.

A year later, the approval was expanded to cover adults aged 50–59 who are at an increased risk for LRTD caused by RSV. Arexvy is not indicated for use during pregnancy. Pfizer (PFE) and Moderna (MRNA) also market FDA-approved RSV vaccines in the U.S.

data.title: AC Immune GAAP EPS of -CHF0.70, revenue of CHF3.57M
data.description:
• AC Immune press release (ACIU): FY GAAP EPS of -CHF0.70.
  • Revenue of CHF3.57M (-86.9% Y/Y).

data.title: Once Upon a Farm slides after its first post-IPO earnings report
data.description:
Once Upon A Farm (OFRM) reported sales rose 30.1% in Q4 to $64.0M. The company highlighted that the sales growth was driven by broadened distribution, significant increases in household penetration, and top-tier velocity in the categories where it competes. CEO John Foraker said the sales growth showed the powerful underlying momentum around the brand and the trust consumers place in the company's mission-driven approach.

Gross profit was 47.7% of sales for the quarter of 2025, compared to  46.7% of sales in the prior year period. The gross profit improvement was driven by lower trade spend and higher average selling prices. Adjusted EBITDA was $6.6M for the quarter, compared to $2.2M in the prior-year period. The increase was primarily driven by the increase in gross profit partially offset by higher SG&A expenses. GAAP EPS of $0.11 topped the consensus estimate by a penny.

Evercore ISI analyst David Palmer said the firm continues to see Once Upon a Farm (OFRM) as an exciting early-stage food growth story with a wide range of potential stock outcomes based on the potential for 30%+ sales growth in 2027 and potentially beyond and the path to double-digit EBITDA margins.

However, the general sense of sell-side analysts was that investors may be disappointed in the reported margin rate. 

Shares of Once Upon A Farm (OFRM) fell 19.3% in premarket trading to $16.43 vs. the post-IPO range of $18.00 to $27.00.

data.title: Better Home & Finance Holding reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Better Home & Finance Holding press release (BETR): Q4 net loss of $39.92M.
  • Revenue of $44.31M (+77.4% Y/Y) beats by $3.6M.
  • Funded Loan Volume of $1.5 billion, compared to $936 million in Q4 2024 and $1.2 billion in Q3 2025, demonstrating 56% growth year over year
  • Approximately 4,293 Funded Loans, compared to 3,326 in Q4 2024 and 4,086 in Q3 2025, demonstrating 29% growth year over year
  • Continued to improve D2C marginal unit economics, expanding D2C marginal per unit contribution margin by 28% quarter-over-quarter
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss of $24 million, compared to a loss of $28 million in Q4 2024 and a loss of $25 million in Q3 2025, demonstrating a 14% improvement year over year

data.title: Heineken sticks with 2030 target as CFO says brewer is driving change amid CEO transition
data.description:
NEW YORK – Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is pressing ahead with its EverGreen 2030 strategy despite an unexpected chief executive transition, with Chief Financial Officer Harold van den Broek telling investors on Thursday that the brewer remains focused on execution, portfolio shifts and tighter capital discipline as it navigates a weak global beer market.

 

Speaking on stage with analyst Sanjeet Aujla at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference, Broek said the search for a successor to outgoing CEO Dolf van den Brink is being led by the supervisory board, which he called its top priority, but stressed that the management team is not pausing operations while that process unfolds.

“For us, internally in the company, this is not a moment to start questioning,” Broek said. “We have a very good strategy.”

He said Heineken’s EverGreen 2030 plan, unveiled at its capital markets day in October, remains intact and is guiding the brewer’s push to improve growth, sharpen resource allocation and modernize its commercial and digital capabilities.

The price of Heineken’s American Depositary Receipts has declined 6.6% in the past 12 months.

Cautious guidance reflects beer market weakness, macro uncertainty

Broek said Heineken’s forecast for 2% to 6% organic EBIT growth this year, below its historical 4% to 8% range, reflects both macroeconomic uncertainty and the reality that consumer sentiment has not yet caught up with the turn of the calendar.

He said the global beer market contracted by about 2% to 2.5% in 2025, and that backdrop shaped management’s decision to set a more cautious range. Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is also shifting from a single annual operating plan to what Broek described as “trigger event planning,” which allows the company to respond more dynamically to changing conditions and growth opportunities.

Even so, he said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) remains confident in the factors it can control, including market share gains, growth in nonalcoholic beer and cost savings to fund investment and profitability.

Strategy shifts target market segmentation, global brands, AI

Broek said a major part of EverGreen 2030 is a more selective approach to market investment. Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) historically treated its roughly 80 operating companies more evenly, he said, but now plans to allocate resources more intentionally based on each market’s growth potential and stage of development.

That means differentiating between value beer markets, advancing beer markets where premiumization drives growth and developed markets where multi-beverage occasions require a broader portfolio approach.

He also said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) sees room to expand the role of global brands. Today, only the Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) brand is managed as a truly global brand, representing about 25% of the company’s portfolio. Broek said consumer research has identified five brands with the potential to play a global role.

Another priority is “Freddie AI,” Heineken’s in-house digital marketing agency, which Broek said is intended to link data, insights and communications more efficiently and support faster growth.

Digital backbone still early, but benefits start to show

Broek described Heineken’s effort to harmonize fragmented IT and ERP systems as still being in the early stages, but said the brewer is beginning to see gains.

The company’s digital platform is now active in four operating companies, and some data and processes have been moved to more standardized systems. That has already enabled applications such as product recommendation, assortment optimization, working capital insights and sales force prioritization, he said.

Broek added that Heineken’s brewery network is now fully connected, allowing learnings from one market to be applied in another without manual intervention. He also pointed to emerging efficiencies in shared services and the rollout of its internal marketing platform.

Overall, he said the company is about 35% to 40% of the way toward its digital target state.

Revenue management gets bigger role as consumers seek value

Broek said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is operating in a world where consumers are increasingly price conscious, and that has become a defining factor in beer market weakness.

He said revenue management is about more than raising prices. It also includes brand appeal, sponsorships, pack-price architecture and ensuring premium products remain affordable.

“The price-conscious consumer is really looking for is the brand worth the investment,” he said.

Broek said the brewer is trying to institutionalize those capabilities, but admitted Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) still has work to do, rating its current revenue management ability at only about “4.5 out of 10” or “5 out of 10.”

Europe stabilizes on shelf space, but volume recovery may take time

In Europe, Broek said nearly all retailer price negotiations have now been completed, meaning a repeat of last year’s shelf space disputes is not expected. He also said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) had recovered the lost shelf space through recent shelf resets in February and March.

Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) reported 1.4% revenue per hectoliter growth in Europe in 2025, which Broek said was important because the company is trying to price below inflation while still offsetting part of its own cost pressure and funding brand investment.

Still, Broek was cautious on the region’s volume recovery. He pointed to deposit return schemes, weak population growth and subdued market conditions in parts of Eastern Europe as factors that could continue to weigh on beer demand.

He said premium and zero-alcohol segments remain attractive, but mainstream beer also needs renewed focus in a value-seeking environment. Heineken’s portfolio spans premium, mainstream and value segments, which he said gives it flexibility to respond.

Broek said Europe’s operating margins remain about 200 basis points below pre-pandemic levels and acknowledged the current level is unsatisfactory. But he said margins can recover if Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) combines volume stabilization, category investment and productivity gains. He said he believes Europe can return to pre-COVID margin levels over time.

Restructuring pushes ahead with job cuts

Broek also discussed Heineken’s restructuring program, which includes reducing 5,000 to 6,000 roles globally and moving about 3,000 employees into Heineken Business Services.

The company is seeking a leaner head office and more automation, particularly in back-office functions. Broek said the changes have generally been embraced outside Europe, where the dynamics are more complex, but added that teams understand the need for change.

He said the process is being handled with “respect, transparency and care,” while still moving at speed.

Brazil and Mexico remain mixed but important growth engines

In Brazil, Broek said 2025 was a tale of two halves. The company gained market share and saw strong momentum for Heineken and Amstel, but had to correct stock levels in trade channels after what he described as an “own goal.” He also said the beer category weakened in the second half as macroeconomic conditions worsened.

Still, he said trading in December and January was better than expected, with the category stable and Heineken’s business performing well. Heineken and Amstel still have room to grow, he said, though the company is also thinking more broadly about how to build out its Brazilian portfolio.

Broek said the company’s new Passos brewery in Brazil serves both as a network optimization move and as a source of future capacity. It reduces shipping inefficiencies and supports growth in a region with strong demand for Heineken and Amstel.

In Mexico, Broek said Heineken’s business remains strong, though the brewer underperformed at times due to a combination of geographic mix, pricing and social unrest in the north. He said the company pushed pricing too hard in the first half of 2025 in a bid to gain share and later had to correct course.

Over the longer term, however, Broek said Mexico remains a resilient and healthy market. He also highlighted the importance of Heineken’s 17,000-store retail franchise as a strategic asset under new country leadership.

Looking ahead to the World Cup, Broek said the event matters less for a one-time sales spike than for restoring excitement around the beer category and reconnecting consumers with the brand.

Vietnam rebound continues, India seen as major long-term prize

Broek pointed to Vietnam as one of Heineken’s strongest recent turnarounds, citing portfolio changes, channel transformation and market share recovery over the past two years. He said early signs around the Tet holiday were encouraging, especially for premium products, and expressed confidence in Vietnam’s growth outlook for 2026.

He also described India as the largest untapped beer opportunity in the world, though not a quick win. He said category development there will depend on continued regulatory improvements, more modern retail and social changes that are making younger consumers more open to beer.

As those younger cohorts join the workforce and social norms evolve, state governments are becoming more open to reforms such as differentiated excise taxes and better licensing, Broek said. Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF), with close to 50% share in India, is trying to help lead that shift.

Cambodia under review, Africa still offers upside

Broek said Cambodia is one of several markets where Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is reevaluating long-term value creation. The company’s options are to fix the business itself, find a partner or exit, he said.

For now, Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is in discussions with the Cambodian government over excise policy and promotional practices, especially ring-pull promotions that Broek said encourage excessive free beer and undermine responsible consumption.

He warned, however, that patience is not unlimited and said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) will consider alternatives if conditions do not improve.

In Africa, Broek said Nigeria was the main drag on performance last year, while Ethiopia, South Africa and Egypt were stronger. He said the Nigerian business is growing share and lowering its breakeven point, but macroeconomic hardship is pushing consumers out of the category.

He added that easing inflation and greater currency stability in Nigeria offer reasons for cautious optimism, though external shocks remain a risk.

In South Africa, Broek said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is seeing real recovery in beer and cider after the Distell acquisition, though wine remains weaker. He said integrating the two businesses took longer than expected, but customer relationships and route-to-market improvements are now beginning to pay off.

Return on capital moves to center stage

Broek said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is putting a much stronger emphasis on return on invested capital, including embedding it into long-term incentives and management dashboards for the company’s top 150 leaders.

He said that when he joined five years ago, the immediate focus was on cost productivity, but the company is now shifting to capital productivity as a new priority.

That includes tighter working capital management, more disciplined capital spending and closer scrutiny of assets. Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) expects capital expenditures to drift closer to 7% of revenue from around 8% as major brewery, digital and sustainability investments peak and begin to normalize.

Broek said the brewer also sees at least 500 million euros of additional working capital opportunity, particularly in inventories and payables.

Growth remains the central message

Asked what investors may still be underestimating, Broek said the answer is the pace of change inside Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) and the long-term growth potential of beer.

He said the company wants to be seen as a growth business, even if that was not evident in 2025, and believes there is still room to expand the category through affordable pricing, innovation and segment development such as zero-alcohol beer.

He also said Heineken (HEINY) (HINKF) is moving faster internally than many may realize, driven by a clearer sense of what needs to change and a workforce that is increasingly willing to embrace that change.

“Heineken is in motion,” Broek said.

data.title: Lucid impresses analysts with its 'ambitious' midsize EV plans
data.description:
Lucid Group (LICD) issued several key announcements at its Investor Day event. The electric vehicle maker highlighted that its upcoming midsize platform will underpin three new electric vehicles aimed at the core of the electric vehicle market, with a starting price around $50K to compete directly with Tesla's (TSLA) Model 3 and Model Y and other mass-market EV offerings. Management also said it sees a path to being cash flow positive by the late decade. 

RBC Capital analyst Tom Narayan said the firm likes the general direction that Lucid (LCID) is now taking. "On the positive side, we applaud Lucid for its ambitious midsize program. It is smart to target the meat of the US auto market, where brand recognition is less important," he updated. "We think a key obstacle Lucid has had in selling its premium vehicles is that it is an unknown brand going up against the likes of Porsche and Mercedes," he added. Lucid (LCID) up against the Tesla (TSLA) Model Y and the Toyota (TM) RAV4 4 is seen as making more sense, especially if the car can boast better battery range relative to price. The firm has a Sector Perform rating on Lucid (LCID).

Morgan Stanley analyst Andrew Percoco said Lucid's (LCID) innovative midsize platform supports scaling to being free cash flow positive, aided by autonomy partnerships that expand the brand and diversify revenue. "However, weak EV demand and execution risk in a competitive market could challenge the near-term cash flow outlook," he warned. The firm kept an Underweight rating in place.

Shares of Lucid (LCID) rose 1.1% in premarket trading on Friday after shedding 7.9% on Thursday.

data.title: AMC Robotics, HIVE Digital partner to advance AI-driven robotics compute infrastructure
data.description:
• AMC Robotics (AMCI) shares fell over 5% premarket on Friday after the robotics solutions provider announced a strategic collaboration with HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE) to advance next-generation AI-driven robotics applications and scalable infrastructure capabilities.
  • Through this collaboration, AMC Robotics has begun utilizing HIVE’s GPU AI compute infrastructure and related services to support its expanding development, testing, and deployment needs. In parallel, the two companies are actively exploring broader areas of cooperation, including potential collaboration across AI optimization, data processing, and infrastructure scalability to support future product initiatives.
  • The companies emphasized that the collaboration is expected to evolve over time as HIVE scales its global infrastructure and AMC Robotics moves toward production deployment. Any future arrangements would be subject to further evaluation and mutually agreed terms.
  • HIVE +3.8% premarket to $2.16
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Costco CFO says consumers remain steady as value focus, digital growth, global expansion support momentum
data.description:
NEW YORK – Costco (COST) sees a consumer who remains willing to spend when the value is clear, even as shoppers navigate uncertainty around tariffs, calendars, gas prices and the broader economy, Chief Financial Officer Gary Millerchip said in a livestreamed interview Thursday at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference.

Speaking to UBS analyst Michael Lasser, Millerchip said Costco’s (COST) business has held up with notable consistency over the past year, despite the shifting headlines that have shaped investor and consumer sentiment.

“When you strip out the different noise elements,” he said, members have been “remarkably consistent,” continuing to spend on both staples and discretionary items when Costco (COST) delivers strong value, quality and fresh merchandise.

That resilience has helped reinforce Costco’s (COST) standing as one of the most closely watched names in retail, especially as other companies offer more mixed assessments of consumer health.

Millerchip said Costco’s (COST) members still appear to have spending capacity, but that the burden remains on the retailer’s merchants and operators to keep earning those dollars. In nonfoods especially, he said, shoppers keep responding when they find compelling prices and a sense of discovery.

Costco's (COST) stock this year is up 16%, contrasting with a 2.5% decline for the Standard & Poor's 500 stock index (SP500).

Gas business remains a loyalty and traffic engine

Costco’s (COST) fuel business continues to work in both high and low price environments, though in different ways, Millerchip said.

When gas prices rise, he said, Costco (COST) benefits from a stronger perception of price advantage, which can encourage members to drive farther to fill up. When prices fall, members may have more discretionary income to spend inside the warehouse, even if some are less motivated to make a special trip for fuel alone.

About half of Costco (COST) gas transactions result in a warehouse visit during the same trip, Millerchip said, underscoring the strategic role gas plays in driving both traffic and loyalty.

He added that Costco (COST) is working to improve congestion and throughput at both warehouses and fuel stations through technology and expansion projects. Among those efforts are checkout improvements, gas station expansions, new pump technology and geofencing initiatives intended to speed visits.

Long runway for growth

Asked about the durability of Costco’s (COST) recent success, Millerchip pointed to several avenues for continued growth, including merchandising, digital capabilities and international store openings.

He said Costco (COST) is still finding ways to add value to membership, citing benefits such as extended shopping hours for executive members and Instacart credits. The goal, he said, is to make joining or renewing a Costco (COST) membership an easy financial decision.

Millerchip also said e-commerce remains a meaningful growth opportunity. While digital sales still make up a relatively small portion of Costco’s (COST) total revenue, he said the company sees room to improve assortment, technology and the online member experience.

He highlighted big and bulky delivery as one area where Costco can differentiate itself, noting that services such as delivery, installation and haul-away can add to the value proposition for members buying large items.

On physical expansion, Millerchip said Costco (COST) still sees a path to opening at least about 30 warehouses a year over the next 5 to 10 years, with opportunities across the U.S. and in international markets including Europe, Asia, Australia, Mexico and Canada.

He said Costco (COST) isn’t focused on entering a new country immediately, but left open the possibility of future geographic expansion beyond its current footprint.

Traffic dips are watched, but not overread

Millerchip said visits remain an important measure of business health, but cautioned against drawing broad conclusions from any single month.

He noted that weather disruptions, especially in the Northeast, affected some recent traffic patterns, including temporary warehouse closures. Even so, he said Costco (COST) did not see anything in the latest month that called for a sharp reaction.

Rather than responding to short-term swings, Millerchip said Costco (COST) remains focused on continuously improving value and reducing friction in the shopping experience.

That includes efforts to increase warehouse efficiency, improve checkout speed, expand capacity in busy locations and keep basket sizes healthy. He said the company is seeing larger baskets driven more by item growth and mix than by inflation, which supports Costco’s efficiency-driven business model.

Digital strategy broadens, but stays true to the brand

Millerchip acknowledged that Costco (COST) has become more aggressive in digital commerce after historically taking a cautious approach.

He said part of that shift reflects work the company has done to modernize its technology foundation. But he stressed that Costco (COST) still views the warehouse as central to the brand and believes physical retail works when merchants give customers a reason to visit.

For Costco (COST), e-commerce is meant to complement the warehouse rather than replace it, he said. That means a curated online assortment, not an attempt to be everything to everyone.

Costco’s (COST) digital business is growing faster than the rest of the company, Millerchip said, and management believes it is largely incremental rather than cannibalistic. He said even services such as same-day grocery delivery through Instacart generally add trips instead of merely shifting them online.

The company has also become more comfortable with personalization, he said, as members increasingly expect more relevant communications and digital experiences.

AI seen as opportunity, not threat

Millerchip said Costco(COST) views artificial intelligence, including large language models and agentic shopping, more as an opportunity than a risk.

Because Costco (COST) doesn’t rely on paid search as a marketing model, he said the company is focused on making sure its value proposition is accurately represented in AI-driven search and recommendation tools.

That means working with major AI platforms to improve data quality, product descriptions and visibility so that Costco (COST) shows up appropriately when consumers search for items such as washers, dryers or other high-ticket products.

Millerchip said Costco (COST) believes AI could help the company surface more often in product discovery because of its pricing, reviews and service advantages, such as free delivery, installation and haul-away on certain items.

Inside the business, he said Costco (COST) is also using AI to improve productivity, especially in areas like call center operations. But he suggested that most of the economic benefit from those efficiencies would likely be reinvested into the business and member experience rather than allowed to simply expand margins.

“We’re primarily a top-line company,” Millerchip said, describing reinvestment as a core part of Costco’s (COST) flywheel.

Digital signups reshape membership mix

Millerchip also addressed a topic closely watched by investors: membership renewal trends.

He said Costco (COST) hasn’t seen a meaningful effect from its recent membership fee increase. Instead, changes in renewal rates have been driven more by a changing mix of members, especially a larger share of digitally acquired shoppers.

Where digital signups once represented less than 5% of new memberships, they now account for more than 40%, Millerchip said. He added that nearly half of new members are under 40, helping lower the average age of Costco’s (COST) membership base and supporting faster overall member growth.

Those digitally acquired members tend to renew at lower rates initially because they are less engaged than members who sign up in warehouses and shop more frequently, he said. But Costco (COST) has been working to improve those outcomes through better onboarding, communication and renewal efforts.

Millerchip said recent trends suggest some stabilization, and he expressed confidence that the company can keep improving digital member engagement over time.

Conviction remains high

Nearly two years into his tenure as CFO, Millerchip said his confidence in Costco’s (COST) long-term model has only strengthened.

He said the company’s founding principles remain as relevant today as they were decades ago: deliver strong value to members, take care of employees and produce good outcomes for shareholders as a result.

At the same time, he said Costco (COST) has shown it can evolve, whether through gas, fresh food, private label, digital investments or new technologies.

For Millerchip, that balance between consistency and adaptation is what supports Costco’s (COST) staying power.

“The company has stayed true to those principles, but it’s evolved over time,” he said. “The principles that create value are absolutely as true today.”
